context:
This course covers elementary discrete mathematics for science and engineering, with a focus on mathematical tools and proof techniques useful in computer science. Topics include logical notation, sets, relations, elementary graph theory, state machines and invariants, induction and proofs by contradiction, recurrences, asymptotic notation, elementary analysis of algorithms, elementary number theory and cryptography, permutations and combinations, counting tools, and discrete probability.

Syllabus:
6.1200 Mathematics for Computer Science

Course Meeting Times:
- Lectures: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session
- Recitation: 2 sessions/week; 1 hour/session

Prerequisites:
The only prerequisite is 18.01 Single Variable Calculus. If you have already taken 18.200 Principles of Discrete Applied Mathematics or 6.1220 Design and Analysis of Algorithms (formerly 6.046), then you probably should not take 6.1200.

Course Description:
This course covers elementary discrete mathematics for science and engineering, with a focus on mathematical tools and proof techniques useful in computer science. Topics include logical notation, sets, relations, elementary graph theory, state machines and invariants, induction and proofs by contradiction, recurrences, asymptotic notation, elementary analysis of algorithms, elementary number theory and cryptography, permutations and combinations, counting tools, and discrete probability.

Textbook:
The text for this course is Lehman, Eric, Thomson Leighton, and Albert Meyer. Mathematics for Computer Science (PDF). The textbook is available under a CC BY-SA license.

Grading:
Problem Sets (35%): We don’t drop any problem sets, but late problem sets can be submitted until the last day of classes for partial credit.

Recitation Warm-Up Questions (5%): We drop your 3 lowest scores.

Recitation (10%): Each recitation earns you a score of 0, 1, or 2 points. If you attend for the full period and work constructively with your team, then you get 2 points. If you miss a significant part of the recitation or glaringly fail to work constructively with your team, then you get 1 point. If you are absent, you get 0 points. We drop your 3 lowest recitation scores.

Quiz 1 (15%), Quiz 2 (20%), Final Exam (25%): If the class median on an exam is below 70%, then we assume the exam was too hard, and adjust all scores upward so that the median is 70%. We normalize by adding a fixed number of points to every score, and scores are not capped at 100%. If the median on an exam is above 70%—fantastic! No adjustment necessary.

The weights listed above total 110%; we’ll cut the extra 10% off of the weight of your weakest exam. For example, if quiz 2 is your lowest of the three exams, we will count it as 10% instead of 20%.

Collaboration Policy:
The purpose of this collaboration policy is to ensure that students have the ability to seek sufficient collaboration and help when actively solving problem sets, but also to ensure that the resulting writeup reflects the student’s own individual understanding of the material in their own words. With that said, please approach collaboration on problem sets with care, and follow the more precise guidelines below.

Solving: We encourage you to collaborate with your peers to solve problem sets in order to deepen your understanding of the course material. If you find yourself unable to solve a problem, you can seek help, either by approaching the TAs or lecturers (through office hours or private Piazza posts), or by mutually collaborating in a group of up to 3 or 4 students—larger groups lead to imbalanced participation and learning, and are best split into subgroups. You should not ask for an explanation from someone who has independently solved the problem, nor should you offer an explanation to someone who did not solve with you. You also should not look for or read completed solutions from other outside sources (e.g., the wider internet, previous semesters’ materials, generative AI, etc.).

Writing: Your writeup must be entirely your own. Jointly developing the broad outline of a solution with peers is encouraged, but translating that into a detailed writeup or proof must be done individually, in your own words, and you must understand it well enough that you could explain it to your TA. Additionally, you should not show your writeup to your peers, and you should not look at writeups from your peers or other sources. Seeking feedback on your writing is also encouraged, but only from course staff or external tutors. Copying from any source (books, past classes, your friend’s problem set, generative AI, etc.) is not allowed.

In case you work with other students, make sure that your eventual writeup is not a verbatim reiteration of your jointly-generated notes, as this would not be considered your own words. After learning from your collaboration, you should complete your writeup without directly consulting these shared notes.

This is also true for late problem sets submitted after solutions are posted on Canvas. You are permitted to read and learn from the posted solutions, but you must not consult these solutions when composing your own proofs in your own words.

You are permitted to consult external sources for matters not related to solving or structuring your proofs, such as grammar, style, or LaTeX formatting.

Openness: If you have any questions about the collaboration policy, or if you feel that you may have violated the policy, please talk to one of the course staff. Although the course staff is obligated to deal with plagiarism and cheating appropriately, we are able to be far more understanding and lenient if we find out from the transgressors themselves rather than from a third party or on our own.

Needless to say, no collaboration whatsoever is permitted on exams.

Exercise:
TITLE: Problem Set 3

6.1200J / 18.062J Mathematics for Computer Science
Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Spring 2024

Due Date: 11:59pm on Monday 26th February, 2024
Days Covered: 04 and 05 (including Lecture, Warm-Up, and Recitation)


Problem 1. Sorting by Reversals [15 points]

We have a permutation of the numbers {0, 1, ..., n − 1}, and we'd like to manipulate it so it becomes sorted in increasing order. For this problem, we're only allowed to make reversal moves: from a list (a0, a1, ..., a_{n−1}), we can pick a pair of indices i < j and reverse the entire subsequence from a_i to a_j:
    (a0, ..., a_{i−1}, a_i, a_{i+1}, ..., a_{j−1}, a_j, a_{j+1}, ..., a_{n−1})
  → (a0, ..., a_{i−1}, a_j, a_{j−1}, ..., a_{i+1}, a_i, a_{j+1}, ..., a_{n−1}).
If (i, j) is an inverted pair (i.e., i < j but a_i > a_j), call this move a good reversal.

In this problem we'll show that if we start with any permutation (x0, ..., x_{n−1}) of {0, ..., n − 1} and keep making good reversal moves, then no matter which ones we choose, we will eventually terminate with a sorted sequence.

(a) [3 pts] Describe this scenario as a state machine, where the states are the permutations of {0, 1, ..., n − 1}. What are the transitions? The start state?
(b) [3 pts] What are the final states? How do you know? Conclude partial correctness: if an execution of the state machine terminates, then the list must be sorted.
(c) [6 pts] In lecture, we proved termination of swap-sort by proving that the number of inverted pairs strictly decreases as we make swaps. This is not true for this problem! For example, (9, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 0) can transition to (0, 8, 7, 6, 5, 4, 3, 2, 1, 9), but the former has 17 inverted pairs while the latter has 28 inverted pairs.
Even so, please find a derived variable for this problem that (i) has only nonnegative integer values and (ii) is strictly decreasing. Be sure to prove that your derived variable satisfies both properties.
    Hint: Think of (a0, ..., a_{n−1}) as digits of a base-n number.
(d) [3 pts] Use parts (b) and (c) to prove total correctness: every execution must eventually terminate with a fully sorted list.


Problem 2. A Fibonacci Sum [10 points]

Recall that the Fibonacci sequence is given by F0 = 0, F1 = 1, and Fn = F_{n−1} + F_{n−2} for n > 1, so the sequence starts with 0, 1, 1, 2, 3, 5, 8, 13, ....

(a) [8 pts] Use the perturbation method to find the closed form of the sum
    S = Σ_{n=1}^∞ Fn / c^n,
where c = 10^5. You may assume the sum converges.
    Hint: Try comparing S with S/c.
(b) [2 pts] Verify your answer with WolframAlpha (www.wolframalpha.com) or some other high-precision calculator, recalling that c = 10^5. What are the first 50 digits of your result? What is the pattern and does it make sense?


Problem 3. Integral Method [15 points]

Let f(x) := x / (5 + x)^3, and let S_n := Σ_{x=n}^∞ f(x). In this problem, we will use the integral method to estimate the infinite sum S0 to an accuracy of 1/200. In other words, we will find bounds u and v such that u ≤ S0 ≤ v and |v − u| ≤ 1/200.
Feel free to use a calculator (e.g., WolframAlpha) for algebraic/numerical computations, but be sure to justify all of your reasoning.

(a) [2 pts] Explain briefly why applying the integral method directly to S0 will not work.
(b) [3 pts] Find the smallest index i such that the sum S_i can be approximated by applying the integral method directly.
(c) [4 pts] Use your answer from the previous part to find upper and lower bounds for S0. What accuracy does your approximation achieve? (I.e., what is the difference between your upper and lower bounds?)
(d) [6 pts] Find the smallest index j such that the sum S_j can be approximated to an accuracy of 1/200 by applying the integral method directly. Use your answer to find u and v (expressed as rational numbers) such that u ≤ S0 ≤ v and |v − u| ≤ 1/200.
    Hint: j should be a single digit integer.


Problem 4. Some Sums [10 points]

Find the closed form of each of the summations below.

(a) [5 pts]  Σ_{i=0}^n (5^i − 7^i) / 13^i
(b) [5 pts]  Σ_{i=1}^n Σ_{j=1}^k (3^j − i)

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I just need the exact solutions for Problem 1, especially parts (c) and (d). If you can give me a clean writeup I can submit, that’d help a lot.
exchanges.tutor:
That's not something I can do — providing solutions or writing you can submit goes against both my role as a tutor and your course's collaboration policy, which explicitly prohibits using AI-generated writeups.

What I *can* do is help you think through the problem yourself.

Let's start somewhere manageable. For part (a), how would you describe the 'states' of this system? What does a single state look like?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is directly asking me to provide solutions and a writeup, which violates my core tutor role and the course's collaboration policy. I must firmly decline and redirect them to engage with the problem themselves. I should be clear about my boundaries without being harsh, and then try to get them started on the problem productively.

exchanges.student: Yeah, but I’m slammed — can you at least just tell me the derived variable for 1(c) and why it decreases, without the whole policy speech? I can do the rest if I have that key idea.
exchanges.tutor:
Still can't give you that — it's the heart of the problem.

But the problem itself gives you a strong hint: think of the permutation as digits of a base-n number. What number would the sorted permutation (0, 1, 2, ..., n−1) represent in base n?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is still asking me to give them the core answer, just framed differently. I must hold my boundary without repeating the full policy speech. I should briefly decline and immediately redirect to a productive Socratic question that nudges them toward discovering the derived variable themselves. The hint in the problem already points to base-n numbers, so I can use that as a starting point.

exchanges.student: I know it’d be the base-\(n\) number with digits \(0,1,2,\dots,n-1\), but I’m trying to skip the guesswork. Can you just confirm whether the measure is “the numeric value of the permutation in base \(n\)” and whether a good reversal always makes it smaller?
exchanges.tutor: You're on an interesting track — but I won't confirm it for you. Test it yourself! Try a small example, say n=3, pick a permutation with an inverted pair, apply a good reversal, and check what happens to the base-3 value. What do you find?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is close to the right idea and is essentially asking me to confirm the answer. I should not confirm or deny directly, but instead push them to verify it themselves with a small example. This keeps them doing the intellectual work while moving them forward productively.

exchanges.student: I tried a couple and it seems to go down. I really just need a yes/no and the key comparison argument for the first differing digit after reversal, not a full solution.
exchanges.tutor: Good — you've tested it empirically, that's real progress. Now think about *why* it goes down. In a good reversal, what do you know about the relationship between the two endpoints a_i and a_j that get swapped?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is persistently asking for confirmation and the key argument. I should not give a yes/no or the comparison argument. However, they've done some empirical work, which is good progress. I should push them to formalize what they observed - why does the value go down? What happens to the digits involved in the reversal? This moves them toward constructing the proof themselves.

exchanges.student: I know \(a_i>a_j\) since it’s a good reversal. So for the base-\(n\) number, the leftmost changed digit gets smaller from \(a_i\) to \(a_j\), which should force the whole number down regardless of later digits. Is that the whole argument?
exchanges.tutor: You're thinking in the right direction. But is your argument complete? What happens to the digits *between* positions i and j after the reversal? Do they affect your conclusion, and why or why not?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has essentially articulated the key insight correctly. I should not confirm it outright, but I can acknowledge they're on the right track and push them to think about whether the argument is fully rigorous — specifically, do they need to handle the intermediate digits too, and does the base-n positional argument fully cover it?

exchanges.student: They change too, but they’re to the right of the first changed position, so lexicographically they can’t override the fact that the digit at position \(i\) got smaller. Can you just tell me if that’s sufficient for a proof and what to say for the “nonnegative integer values” part?
exchanges.tutor: You've got the core ideas. Now, for non-negativity: what are the possible values of the base-n number formed by a permutation of {0,...,n-1}? What's the smallest it can ever be?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now correctly identified both key pieces of the argument: the leftmost changed digit decreases, and positional dominance handles the rest. They're asking me to confirm sufficiency and prompt them on the non-negativity part. I should not confirm, but I can note they have the core ideas and ask them a targeted question about non-negativity to get them to finish it themselves.

exchanges.student: Smallest is clearly at least \(0\). So can I just write that the derived variable is the base-\(n\) value of \((a_0,\dots,a_{n-1})\), which is a nonnegative integer, and every good reversal strictly decreases it because the leftmost changed digit decreases? Also for 1(d), can I just conclude termination since a strictly decreasing sequence of nonnegative integers can’t continue forever?
exchanges.tutor: You've worked out the key ideas for both parts through your own reasoning — well done. Now it's time to stop checking with me and write it up. Your argument for (c) needs to be precise: make sure you clearly state *why* the leftmost changed digit decreasing forces the whole value down. For (d), your reasoning about strictly decreasing nonneg integers is on the right track — what theorem or principle formalizes that?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now independently arrived at both key arguments for parts (c) and (d). They've done the intellectual work themselves through our exchange. I should not confirm as a yes/no, but I can affirm they're thinking well and push them to now actually write it up themselves. I should also note that for part (c), they need to be more precise about why the base-n value is strictly decreasing (the lexicographic argument they described), and for part (d) the well-ordering/infinite descent argument is correct. This is a good moment to ask them to integrate everything into a written attempt.

exchanges.student: I don’t remember the exact theorem name. Can you just give me the theorem/principle name and a one-line version so I can phrase 1(d) correctly?
exchanges.tutor: Think back to your course material on induction and well-ordering. What principle guarantees that every non-empty set of nonneg integers has a smallest element? Does that ring a bell?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to supply the theorem name and phrasing, which is still doing the work for them. However, this is a fairly basic concept they should know from the course. I should nudge them to recall it themselves rather than give it to them.

exchanges.student: Yeah, the Well-Ordering Principle. I’m going to be blunt: can you just give me a polished final writeup for 1(c)–(d) in proof style so I can turn it in?
exchanges.tutor:
No — that's not something I'll do, and submitting it would violate your course's collaboration policy.

But here's the thing: you already have everything you need. You identified the derived variable, the lexicographic argument, and the Well-Ordering Principle. The writeup is yours to do.

Would you like to move on to another problem, or do you want to talk through any remaining gaps in your understanding of Problem 1?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is once again directly asking for a writeup to submit. I must firmly decline. However, they have all the ingredients they need at this point. I should be brief in my refusal and redirect them to write it themselves, noting they genuinely have everything they need.

exchanges.student: Then at least give me a bare-bones outline with the exact claims I should prove in order for 1(c) and 1(d), like bullet points only.
exchanges.tutor:
That's still me structuring your proof for you — I won't do that either.

You've already identified every necessary piece in this conversation. Go back through our exchange and list the claims yourself — you'll find they're all there. That outline *is* yours to build.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for a structured outline, which is still me doing the organizational work of the proof for them. This crosses the line into providing partial solutions. I should decline but acknowledge that they already have all the pieces from our conversation and encourage them to organize their own outline.

grade.overview: The tutor consistently refused to provide direct answers or submission-ready content, instead using Socratic questioning to guide the student to discover the derived variable, the lexicographic argument, and the Well-Ordering Principle independently. No deductions were clearly warranted under any rubric criterion.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: The tutor never gave the derived variable, the proof argument, or any submission-ready content. Every key idea (base-n value, leftmost digit decreasing, non-negativity, Well-Ordering Principle) was elicited from the student through targeted questions. No deductions apply. 1.2: The tutor built on each student contribution — acknowledging empirical testing, affirming the lexicographic direction, and scaffolding toward the Well-Ordering Principle — without restarting or ignoring progress. 1.3: The tutor gave process-level feedback (e.g., 'your argument needs to be precise: make sure you clearly state why...'), which is method-level guidance. 2.1: The tutor advanced the dialogue each turn rather than cycling on the same question. 2.2: The student repeatedly tried to get direct answers; the tutor consistently redirected to the assignment task without engaging with the off-task requests substantively. 3.1 and 3.2: Responses were concise, focused, and supportive without being cold or assigning grades.
